sources_audited.note: The BLS matrix supports the stated 2024 and 2034 employment levels, 9.9% growth, and cited occupation shares for the combined private business-schools/computer-and-management-training industry. It is not 611420-specific, as the packet discloses.

The Anthropic report supports the O*NET task mapping, roughly 37-40% computer-and-mathematical conversation share, and 49.1% automation share. These are selected Claude-use observations, not industry implementation evidence, which the packet acknowledges.

The cited Census URL opens the NAICS search page, but its rendered search results contain no 611420 definition or the claimed activity and delivery-mode details. The citation therefore does not support its stated claim at the cited location.

The BLS Occupational Outlook Handbook page states that computer and IT occupations should grow much faster than average from 2024 to 2034 and projects about 317,700 openings per year. It measures occupations rather than computer-training demand, as the bridge discloses.

NCES supports the stated fall-2021 undergraduate figures of 61% taking at least one distance course and 28% studying exclusively at a distance. Those pandemic-era degree-granting-postsecondary figures do not by themselves establish enterprise computer-training self-service, operator elimination, or retention of AI savings.

The June 2026 Anthropic report supports a linked sample of about 9,700 survey respondents and the roughly 30% versus 4% computer-and-mathematical occupation comparison, while explicitly warning that the survey is not representative of the general population.

caveats: S3 does not render the claimed 611420 definition at the cited URL; removing it would not change the honest ESTIMATE states for e and q or the disclosed PROXY state for o, so this source defect is not material under the required tier/action test.
S5 establishes participation in remote postsecondary education in fall 2021, not AI-driven self-service substitution or retention of labor savings in employer computer training.

The d5 bridge converts combined-industry employment growth and end-occupation openings into service-quantity growth; the transformation is disclosed and directionally plausible but remains broad and indirect.

The Claude-use evidence in S2 and S6 is strongly selected toward computer and mathematical users and does not measure adoption, implementation success, or automatable work shares inside target-sized 611420 operators.

The recurring-employer lens is disclosed and coherent, but the central e, s5, q, and rho bases remain operator-specific judgments without representative firm classification, transfer, contract, or deployment data; the MODEL_CONDITIONED readiness, broad interval, and VALIDATE_ASSUMPTIONS action correctly reflect that limitation.
summary: Mechanics pass. The narrowed recurring-employer lens is explicit and coherent, construct semantics are not reversed or double counted, evidence states are honest, ranges expose substantial uncertainty, and the narrative follows the registered driver and weakness. S3 is unsupported at the cited location and S5 is only a weak bridge, while the other sources support their stated facts but remain broad proxies. Correcting or removing those citations would not change the existing ESTIMATE/PROXY classifications, the PRIORITY base tier, or the VALIDATE_ASSUMPTIONS action, so there are no material findings and the record is publishable with caveats.
